author: @darklordojeda
text: I used to call them egg beaters or "opties" because of the optical illusion that the turbine was spinning one way and the shaft was spinning the opposite direction.

author: @Allan_aka_RocKITEman
text:
​@RobertMurraySmith >>> FWIW: I know there is an issue with HAWTs being more efficient overall than VAWTs. 

That said, _aesthetically_ VAWTs just have that _COOL FACTOR_ that HAWTs do not equal.

author: @brettlarsen6111

author: @ArcanusLibero

author: @brotusclips
text:
so can you think of the functionality of the Darrieus-type designs as similar to a wind-driven flywheel? one that converts the force of wind into torque then uses its wide center of mass to maintain its inertia? meaning the concept of a wind-driven flywheel alternator isn't an affront to the laws of hydrodynamics?
I've been blowing thru youtube videos on VAWTs and flywheels trying to see if anyone has attempted to use a VAWT to drive a flywheel alternator to generate a lower but more consistent charge over a much longer period of time, even using magnets to keep the flywheel balanced with minimal friction. if you, my local expert, can confirm that the Darrieus-type turbine functions similarly to a flywheel, then I'll keep staying up way too late trying to find other definitive proof of my idiocy. your video on the flywheel alternator made me more concerned that I was on to something - really solid engineering work that.
The closest thing I've seen to a VAWT-driven flywheel in the market is the powerpod which has a short circular array of blades, and I've found one or two engineers whom'st seem less than confident in the design philosophy (and believe the media hype is borderline fraudulent). since I'm just a curious DIYer seeking info from local experts I tend towards the side of skepticism, but I also don't think the design tried to solve actual fundamental problems with small-scale wind energy like maintaining a current that can be used by a standard charge controller without a supercapacitor bank for your battery bank so u can charge b4 u charge. I just need some expert's confirmation that I am or am not a loon

author: @robertlackey7212
text: To me the biggest advantage of the vertical axis wind mills is that you can get more energy for a given amount of land , so it you have a  small raised plateau and want to get the maximum amount of power from it , the vertical axis wind mills more closely  spaced is the best choice.

author: @mitobi
text: A company from swiss is actually testing a prototype in Germany with a power of more than 100kw, as far as i remember they would go above 1 MW in the final version.

author: @maximosh
text:
A mechanical centrifugal clutch could apply braking at high velocity wind speeds. 
Limitation on their size can be overcome if they're placed on top of a post with the lower part of the blades elevated.
One could even place support wires on the post if necessary, since the post would be below the blades.

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: Use a car starter and car disk break to fix the starting and high wind breaking issue.  Activated on/off by wind speed.  Also, it can be made with honey cone interior blades and fiberglass.  If you want to get fancy, carbon fiber bade made the same way as fiberglass.  :)

author: @carlvanzelderen
text: What is really a question for me is about the efficiency. It is clear that any VAW is less efficient than a HAW. But, I do have a piece of land on a hill. The wind is very much following the land surface in parallel. I do think one of the advantages of the Darius “egg” type is that wind not coming in from a horizontal angle but from below will be used for power than any HAW . Any thoughts on that matter? By the way speaking of a 2-5kW mill in my ideas. Thanks for any input.

author: @Hellgazer
text: Hey Doc! I heard that the Bettz Law is only valid for non-compressable fluids, and air is a compressable fluid.  So why don't you try a sail like thing repeated like in a circular shape? If you close the upper part of the circle, it would compress the air and gain more thrust. I would try that, but I'm not a handy guy like you.

replies.author: @ДэнКошман
replies.text:
If there is wind at the speed of sound, then air compressibility will appear.
Do you often have a wind of 340 m / s?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea mate - cheers

replies.author: @Hellgazer
replies.text: @ДэнКошман you dont need all that speed for a low compression.

replies.author: @ДэнКошман
replies.text: @Hellgazer At a speed of less than 200 m / s, air compression does not work at all.

replies.author: @Hellgazer
replies.text: @ДэнКошман isn't the air in being compressed against sail winds in a sailling ship? I dont know if I'm using wrong names here, but what I'm talking about is to use the winds  in a circle the same way a sailling ship does in the ocean, got it? Dont you agree that big sail winds are better to gather wind than a rotating stick in a common wind turbine? Just look at the big sailling ships of the old and how many tons of cargo they carried along the Oceans.

author: @scotttovey
text:
The non self start could be resolved by putting cones at the top and bottom of the blades.

Resolving the high wind issue can be done by building a platform that has a sleeve that would move up to surround the blades, blocking the wind a little or a lot and reduce how much wind the blades are catching.

author: @YoureNowOnTV
text: It’s interesting that the H type here in Hobart were mentioned. They were removed because of safety concerns. They were Seagull perches for the majority of their life as they were locked down due to parts failing and a blade fell off early into their life. They failed when it got windy even though the manufacturer rated them for cyclone speed wind. It was great to see an attempt at using wind turbines but it was a joke in its application.

replies.text: I have been to Hobart - believe or not - It reminded me of my home town!

I'll start the draft changes ASAP! 😁👍

author: @ThisTall
text: Would love to see you guys do a rope type wind turbine. Seen a picture of one on a cable spanned across a canyon but never seen a video demo.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: do you. have link mate?

replies.author: @ThisTall
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I’m really sorry, it’s been almost 10 years since I first seen it. Ive looked a few times over the years and can never find it. I thought it was a Popular Mechanics magazine. 
I’m left to assume it must not have worked well because it doesn’t seem to have become popular, but it’s extreme simplicity and claims of high torque and low risk of catastrophic failure seemed very promising. 

All it was, was as many Darrieus fans as you can slide down a cable, and string it across as far a span as you can, or multiple short cables for smaller spans. And the fans were very narrow and not much wider then the cable diameter. But it was suggested that the shorter your cable span was, the bigger the fan diameter. 
The claim was that the potential torque was high enough on a really long span, that you could keep the rpm low for safety.

Sorry I couldn’t send a proper link, but if you have an email, I’m happy to send a few sketches of the different configurations it suggested.
